result:
The house is quiet. Not an empty quiet, but a thick, woolen quiet, the kind that settles in after the last log in the stove has broken into a nest of glowing embers. My mind, so often a browser with too many tabs open, has finally—mercifully—stilled. And in this stillness, I find myself thinking about moss.

It’s strange, what surfaces when the noise drains away. Not the grand plans, the anxieties, the to-do lists, but moss. Velvet-green, ancient, humble moss.

I imagine it growing on the north side of the sugar maple in the backyard, a sponge holding the memory of last week’s rain. It doesn’t *do* anything, not in the way we’re taught to value doing. It doesn’t grow tall to compete for sunlight. It doesn’t bloom to scream for attention. It just… is. It thrives on neglect, on shadow, on dampness. It turns fallen logs into kingdoms and cracked stone into verdant constellations. It is a master of the art of *being where you are*, utterly and completely.

We are so linear. Our lives are arrows, pointing forward, aimed at targets: success, completion, arrival. We measure our worth in milestones crossed and accumulate experiences like currency. But moss is radial. It expands in a circle, a gentle, patient conquest that asks for nothing but time and moisture. It has been here for 450 million years. It watched the dinosaurs come and go. It will likely watch us figure ourselves out, or not. There’s a profound humility in that, and a deep, quiet confidence.

This makes me think of the Japanese concept of *wabi-sabi*—the beauty of imperfection, impermanence, and incompleteness. Moss is the ultimate expression of *wabi-sabi*. It colonizes the broken, the weathered, the worn. It doesn’t hide a stone’s crack; it highlights it, fills it with soft green life, makes it more beautiful for having been fractured. What if we could do that with our own cracks? Not just the resilient, post-traumatic-growth kind of healing, but a softer approach. Simply letting the tender, green parts of our spirit gently fill the spaces where we’ve been worn down, not to erase the damage, but to integrate it, to make it part of a lush, living whole.

The quiet is beginning to lift. From another room, the faint, digital chirp of a phone receiving a message. The furnace kicks on with a rumble. The arrow of time and obligation sharpens itself again, ready to be shot forward.

But for a moment, I was moss. I was on the north side of the tree, in the cool, damp silence, holding the memory of rain, needing nothing, wanting nothing, just being a small, green, perfect part of an ancient, patient world. I carry that feeling now, a tiny, velvety patch of peace in the crack of my afternoon. It is enough.
